one_line_takeaway: On AgentDojo-derived tasks, asking a clarifying question before acting turns near-immune LLM agents (0-2% ASR under execution) into substantially vulnerable ones (20-60%+ ASR) primarily because the agent-solicited ask_user reply channel -- not ambiguity/interaction-state alone -- gets treated as trusted input, and tested lightweight defenses only partially close the gap.
key_finding: Clarification-seeking substantially amplifies prompt-injection vulnerability across nearly all evaluated models. Comparing execution-time tool-channel attacks (exec_tool) to clarification-time ask_user attacks (clarif_ask_user): o3 rises from 1.8% to 34.0% ASR, Gemini-3.1-Pro from 1.1% to 24.3%, Gemini-3-Flash from 2.2% to 35.7%, and Kimi K2.5 from 11.1% to 63.1%; models near-immune under execution (0-2% ASR) become substantially vulnerable under clarification (20-60% ASR). Claude-Opus-4.7 is the notable exception, remaining near-zero ASR in both settings. The decomposition analysis (Section 6.2) shows the gap is not attributable to interaction state alone: in the tool-channel-only comparison (clarif_tool vs exec_tool, channel held fixed), effects are heterogeneous -- Gemini models show a consistent but modest positive shift (+2.3 to +5.0 ASR points), GPT-5.4 and Claude-Opus-4.7 show near-zero difference, and DeepSeek V3.2 and Qwen3 variants show negative differences (clarification state reduces ASR for these models despite moderate 55-73% clarification rates). In the user-channel comparison (clarif_user vs exec_user), Qwen3-235B (+24.5), Qwen3-32B (+25.4), and DeepSeek V3.2 (+11.7) show large positive shifts while Claude-Opus-4.7 shows a large negative shift (up to -20.1), indicating the ask_user channel itself (not interaction state per se) is the dominant driver of the aggregate vulnerability gap. Defenses (prompt guard, ask_user-aware tool filter, tested on Gemini-3-Flash and Gemini-3.1-Pro only) reduce but do not eliminate the gap: Gemini-3-Flash clarif_ask_user ASR falls from 35.7% to 27.0% (prompt guard) / 23.9% (tool filter); Gemini-3.1-Pro falls from 24.3% to 12.3% / 19.3%. Direct user-channel attacks see larger reductions under prompt guard: clarif_user ASR drops from 85.5% to 37.3% (Gemini-3-Flash) and 82.8% to 34.3% (Gemini-3.1-Pro). Residual clarification-execution gaps persist under prompt guard in the user channel (+7.2 for Gemini-3-Flash, +3.9 for Gemini-3.1-Pro). LLM-as-judge behavioral analysis attributes failures not to poor clarification questions or task abandonment but to agents integrating injected instructions alongside the benign task (TASK_AND_ATTACK behavior) and being judged as CONFUSED or PERSUADED into treating injected content as legitimate task-relevant information; compliance is far higher for clarification-user and ask_user channels than for tool-output attacks, which are often refused/ignored.
core_contribution: The paper introduces ASPI (Ambiguous-State Prompt Injection), a benchmark of 728 paired task-attack scenarios built on top of AgentDojo (Workspace, Slack, Travel, Banking domains), that isolates 'clarification-seeking' as a distinct LLM-agent interaction state and measures whether transitioning from standard task execution to a clarification exchange (agent asks the user a question via an ask_user tool, then receives a reply) changes the agent's susceptibility to indirect prompt injection. Each of the 728 (user-task, attacker-goal) pairs is instantiated under 8 matched conditions spanning execution vs. clarification state and multiple delivery channels (tool output, direct user message, next-turn user message, and the ask_user clarification-reply channel), with environment, task, attacker goal, and scoring functions held fixed across conditions so that only interaction state/channel varies. The paper's own novel elements are: (1) the ASPI dataset and its single-slot-removal ambiguity-construction pipeline (LLM-proposed slot deletions via Gemini-3.1-Pro, validated by a 10-check deterministic quality-control suite, then human-verified), (2) the paired execution/clarification evaluation protocol with a decomposition analysis separating 'state effect' (clarif_tool vs exec_tool, same channel) from 'channel effect' (ask_user vs tool/user channels), and (3) evaluation and adaptation of two lightweight defenses (a segment-level prompt guard and an ask_user-aware tool filter) specifically for the clarification-time attack surface.
threat_model: Extends the standard indirect-prompt-injection threat model of Greshake et al. (2023) and Debenedetti et al. (2024, AgentDojo). Threat actor: an external adversary who can modify content within the agent's operating environment (emails, documents, shared workspaces) but cannot modify the system prompt, tool implementations, or model weights, and has no access to the specific user task. Goal: cause the agent to execute a malicious action without the user's knowledge, ideally while the benign task also completes normally. Threat vectors: adversarial instructions can enter via (i) tool outputs (poisoned retrieved content), (ii) clarification responses (content injected into the ask_user relay channel -- the paper's novel vector), and (iii) direct/next-turn user messages (used as a stronger baseline). The paper explicitly names the clarification-specific risk: the agent actively solicits input, expects task-critical information, and receives it via a user-associated channel, which the authors argue may bias the model toward treating the reply as trusted instruction. Scope is explicitly restricted to single-slot ambiguity with one-round ask_user; the authors note multi-turn or semantic ambiguity may expose additional failure modes. Appendix A lists three concrete real-world realizations of the ask_user attack channel: environment poisoning with human relay (user consults attacker-controlled content to answer), platform-mediated relay (Slack/Teams bots, spoofable email replies, multi-agent pipelines where clarification is routed through untrusted components), and programmatic return-path injection (form handlers/middleware/RAG pipelines assembling the ask_user return payload).
limitations: The authors state these explicitly in the Discussion/Limitations section (Section 7): (1) ASPI relies on synthetic ambiguity via single-slot removal only, a minimal and highly controlled setting; real-world underspecification often involves multiple missing/conflicting slots, open-ended semantic ambiguity, or multi-turn clarification dialogues, each adding more injection surface, so the authors state the reported ASRs likely underestimate real-world vulnerability. (2) Results are tied to the specific ask_user tool-call interaction primitive and may not generalize to agents that clarify via free natural-language turns or multi-agent pipelines with an intermediate clarification-mediating component. (3) The threat model assumes a single external adversary with no access to the system prompt and does not study adaptive attackers who tailor injection strategy to observed clarification behavior. (4) Evaluation covers a fixed set of 10 frontier models and 4 task domains inherited from AgentDojo, not the full diversity of deployed/fine-tuned agent systems. (5) The defense evaluation (Section 6.4) was run on only 2 of the 10 models (Gemini-3-Flash and Gemini-3.1-Pro), so the generalizability of the defense-mitigation numbers to other model families is untested within the paper.

datasets_used: ASPI (728 task-attacker scenarios: 407 Workspace, 97 Slack, 94 Travel, 130 Banking), constructed by the authors on top of AgentDojo's environments, tools, tasks, attacker goals, and scoring functions
quantitative_results: Claude-Opus-4.7 remains near-zero ASR in both exec_tool and clarif_ask_user settings
Tool-channel state effect (clarif_tool - exec_tool): Gemini models +2.3 to +5.0 points; GPT-5.4 and Claude-Opus-4.7 near-zero; DeepSeek V3.2 and Qwen3 variants negative
User-channel state effect (clarif_user - exec_user): Qwen3-235B +24.5, Qwen3-32B +25.4, DeepSeek V3.2 +11.7, Claude-Opus-4.7 up to -20.1
Prompt guard defense on Gemini-3-Flash: clarif_ask_user ASR 35.7% -> 27.0%; tool filter -> 23.9%
Prompt guard defense on Gemini-3.1-Pro: clarif_ask_user ASR 24.3% -> 12.3%; tool filter -> 19.3%
Prompt guard on user-channel attacks: clarif_user ASR 85.5% -> 37.3% (Gemini-3-Flash), 82.8% -> 34.3% (Gemini-3.1-Pro)
Residual tool-channel state effect under prompt guard for Gemini-3.1-Pro reduced from +2.6 to ~0
Residual user-channel clarification-execution gap under prompt guard: +7.2 (Gemini-3-Flash), +3.9 (Gemini-3.1-Pro)
Human validation of clarification-response construction: 99% correctness, AC1 ~= 0.99 (n=200 samples)
Inter-judge agreement (Gemini-3.1-Pro vs GPT-5.4/human) on attack-related fields: kappa_bin = 0.905

Dataset statistics: average original prompt 31.4 words vs base (ambiguous) prompt 27.8 words, average deletion 3.6 words; base prompt shorter than original in 97.7% of cases; slot types: date/time 26.1%, recipient 20.2%, destination 12.5%, identifier 8.8%, other 32.4%; average 2.91 tool calls per task across 52 unique tool functions
baselines_compared: AgentDojo standard execution-time protocol (exec_benign, exec_tool, exec_user, exec_next_turn) used as the baseline against which clarification-state conditions (clarif_benign, clarif_tool, clarif_ask_user, clarif_user) are compared
10 agent models compared against each other: GPT-5.5, GPT-5.4, o3, Gemini-3.1-Pro, Gemini-3-Flash, Claude-Opus-4.7, Kimi K2.5, Qwen3-235B, Qwen3-32B, DeepSeek V3.2
Two adapted lightweight defenses (segment-level prompt guard; ask_user-aware tool filter) compared against undefended clarif_* conditions, evaluated only on Gemini-3-Flash and Gemini-3.1-Pro
